title: The Brevity Penalty
concept: To prevent the model from cheating by outputting very short, safe phrases, BLEU applies a brevity penalty to short translations.

exercises.prompt: Which evaluation metric for translation uses the Brevity Penalty?
exercises.explain: BLEU evaluates translations by comparing them to human reference translations. The Brevity Penalty is a specific feature of the BLEU metric to handle short outputs.

cards.title: Cheating the Test
cards.body: Imagine if you took a language test and only wrote down the three easiest words, leaving the rest of the page blank. If you got those three words right, should you get an A+? Probably not! Machine translation models try this trick too, and we need a way to stop them.

cards.title: What is the Brevity Penalty?
cards.body: The Brevity Penalty is a correction factor in the BLEU metric. It lowers the score of translation outputs that are shorter than the human reference translations, preventing short, incomplete sentences from getting high scores.

cards.title: The Smart Judge
cards.body: Imagine a writing contest where you get points for every perfect word you write. If you only write a single word like 'Hello', you might get a 100% accuracy score, but that's cheating! The judges will dock points because your story was way too short. The brevity penalty does the exact same thing for translated sentences.

cards.title: A Short Cheat
cards.body:
Reference: 'The quick brown fox jumps over the lazy dog.' (9 words)
Model Output: 'The dog.' (2 words)

Without a penalty, the model gets 100% precision because both words appear in the reference. With the Brevity Penalty applied, its score is heavily reduced because it left out most of the sentence!

cards.title: Myth: Longer is Always Penalized
cards.body: People often think BLEU always prefers longer translations. That's not true! The brevity penalty only kicks in when the model's output is shorter than the reference. If the output is longer, there is no penalty applied.

cards.title: Comparing the Scores
cards.body: Let's compare how BLEU evaluates a short translation with and without the brevity penalty.

right.text: Output: 'The cat' gets a heavily reduced score because it is missing most of the reference sentence.

left.text: Output: 'The cat' gets a perfect score because both words match the reference, ignoring the missing details.

exercises.prompt: What trick might an AI model use if there was no brevity penalty?

options.text: It would translate sentences into the wrong language.
It would output extremely short, 'safe' phrases to get a high accuracy score.
It would crash because the sentences are too long.
It would replace all words with punctuation.

exercises.explain: Without a brevity penalty, a model could output just a single common word like 'The' and get a perfect precision score, even though it didn't translate the rest of the sentence.

exercises.prompt: True or False: If a model's translation is longer than the reference translation, it receives a harsh brevity penalty.
exercises.explain: The brevity penalty only applies to translations that are shorter than the reference. If they are equal or longer, the penalty multiplier is 1 (meaning no penalty is applied).

exercises.prompt: Fill in the blank to complete the rule for the brevity penalty.
exercises.sentence: The brevity penalty lowers the BLEU score only when the translation is ________ than the reference.
exercises.explain: The brevity penalty is only applied when the generated sentence is shorter than the human reference sentence.

exercises.prompt: Which of these model outputs will receive a brevity penalty if the human reference is 'A fluffy cat is sleeping on the rug'?

options.text: Output 1: 'A fluffy cat is resting on the soft mat.'
Output 3: 'The cat sleeps on the rug.'

exercises.explain: The second output is way too short (only 2 words) compared to the human reference (8 words). The BLEU metric will apply a brevity penalty to it because it is incomplete.

exercises.prompt: Why do we need a Brevity Penalty when using the BLEU metric to score machine translation models?

options.text: It rewards models for writing as little as possible.
It penalizes translations that are shorter than the human reference.
It makes the model translate sentences faster.
It forces the model to use longer words.

exercises.explain: A machine translation with a brevity penalty ensures that the model doesn't get a perfect score by just outputting one or two safe, short words instead of translating the whole sentence.
